sentences.text: It is terribly important for people to realize that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they Jews were pinned in and pitted against such immense powers that there was no possibility [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to know what to do, and certainly there was no possibility for any really meaningful defense or resistance.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is, by and large, true of all of Central and Eastern European Jewry, but it is, I would say, with much greater force true of Hungary.
It is 1944, and the collapse of Germany was reasonably certain by that time, and certainly could be expected.

I certainly was convinced that the war couldn't go on for much longer, and I-- you know, we know from history that most of the allies also saw, so to say, the-- the end of the war, foresaw the victory.
But Hungary was locked in.
There was, for years, no border of Hungary that was not occupied by Germany, or that was free of German-supported and-- and German-guided domestic Nazis.
And there was no way in which you could organize, obtain the means of-- of defense or armament.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was no escape route, really.
At one time, the remaining Jews came to Hungary because it seemed to have been a little better there, and then, after the Romania [INAUDIBLE] war, some of Hungarian Jews ran to Hung-- to Romania.
But there was no-- no outlet.
There was no border.
There were no-- no means of communication.

I had what probably was as good knowledge as could be had before the-- before [INAUDIBLE] and the Slovakian Jews became aware of all the actual details of the Auschwitz annihilation process, which was somewhat later than-- than we were deported already.
But I had a pretty good general knowledge of the tragedy that was-- that have befallen, by that time, most of Central and Eastern European Jewry and-- and that was awaiting us.
In part, that came from simple political consciousness and from following events and from being aware of the world that we lived in.
But it was also buttressed by several [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bits of reliable information-- as reliable as you could get and as much as you could get.
First of all, after the Polish debacle.
At one point, we opened these borders for the Polish army to traverse Hungary after they surrendered their weapons.

And we had managed to, when they were coming across, to contact and spirit away some of the Jewish-- Polish Jewish solders, who had pretty good information on the atrocities that have already taken place.
This was in the very early days, but it was.

And then, bits of information basically filtered through, even through that-- that thick curtain that was drawn around each of the occupied countries and-- or, for that money, each of the Axis countries, and-- and certainly around the-- the Holocaust.
But, you know, the Holocaust was no secret, in essence.
Either to the Jews or to the world at large.

And perhaps therein lies [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one of the more serious elements of the tragedy, that despite the awareness, even if it wasn't quite accurate or the information was incomplete, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] nobody did what should have been done.

How much could have been done, it remains an open question, and-- and certainly is questionable enough not to be very firm about condemning anyone and-- and anybody [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] at least until late '43 or middle of '43 or '44, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when perhaps the Allies could have done a lot more than-- than-- well, I shouldn't say a lot more.
Could have done something, because essentially nothing was done.

Well, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we were all told in our city, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] through placards and through announcements in-- in-- in Hungarian small towns-- there used to be what-- public proclamations.
There was a-- a uniformed man who would go around and bang on a drum and people would collect and he would read-- read out the latest regulations or rules issued by whatever authorities.

And we were told that, one fine morning at 4:00, we all have to be ready and packed and we could take, I don't remember, about 50 kilos of belongings and food to-- with us and to be ready to be moved into the ghetto.
Which was a pretty makeshift affair in-- in our town, in part because it was, you know, a relatively godforsaken, primitive area on the one hand.
And on the other hand, because they knew that it was going to be here for a very brief period.

So essentially all that was done is that one [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] area of this-- on either side of this [INAUDIBLE] street, was designated as the ghetto, and families were, when we were driven out of our homes, we went in there and the family settled in with whomever they could.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was kind of rough and tumble.
Again, you know, unfortunately, human nature [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] doesn't really provide for extraordinary circumstances on the whole.
It was difficult for the people who were moving in with other people, and it was difficult for the people who were to take in other people.
You know, who is going to be sleep in what corner, how are you going to manage to divide this space in which maybe five people lived before and now they're going to have to house 50.

And how are you going to manage to-- to acquire and prepare food, and how are you going to manage to keep yourselves clean and-- and wash and the myriad little details of daily existence, which have become so terribly problematic within a matter of hours, and for which no one had a ready solution because, no one could prepare themselves for it.
And it was very difficult on everybody.

And I do not have to tell you what happens to tempers and nerves under that kind of stress, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which, you know, while everybody tried heroically to be as-- as cooperative as they possibly could, but it-- it really was stretching every nerve and-- and the stress was just overwhelming for everyone.
As I recall, there were some 30 some odd people in this apartment, which, you know, was basically a one-floor small house, and there was an attic.
I remember I sort of got myself organized on-- on the attic and brought some of the young people there.

And it-- it was-- there-- there really wasn't any time even to organize yourself to the point where it-- that was possible because I think it was less than three weeks, maybe, that we were all in the ghetto, and then we went-- deported.
So before, you know, you could kind of organize yourself and establish some kind of a routine and-- and rules of behavior and-- and order, we were gone.
I think it was Shavuot, but I'm not certain.
I-- I really have no recollection of dates and-- and exact timing, although it's recorded and I-- and I've seen it, but I-- I simply don't remember it myself now.
The year was 1944, in May.
It must have been-- you know, we must have been put into the ghetto in the last days of April or the first days of May.
And by May 29, we were deported.
I was 19.
No-- yeah, I was 19 the preceding March.
Well, we talked very little, to tell you the-- the truth, because we were so dang busy.

What little organization, what little provision, what little, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, burying of people, what-- what little attempt to bring medication and some-- whatever kind of [INAUDIBLE] to the-- to the afflicted was-- was us, a few young people.
We were supposed-- we were supposed to be-- I too was in what they called the ghetto police, which was headed by one of the prominent assimilated Jews.

And-- and it shouldn't be-- I shouldn't even call it that, because it had no relationship to what we know about the ghettos of Poland, or the police-- the Jewish police of the ghettos of Poland, and no relationship whatsoever.
But we were sort of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] trying to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not so much maintain order as well as carrying out the orders that were given to the Jews via the console.
And so for instance, they would permit us to take the dead out and bury them in the cemetery, and that enabled us to-- to do certain things by being outside of the ghetto.
You know, like bringing in some food or some medication or-- or through bribery and through chicanery and through whatever we could.
And so there was very little time and opportunity to-- to communication.
Though, in-- in our house, again, it was, you know, a little more so than in the average home as I remember, in that attic, you know, the way we used to [INAUDIBLE] congregate as much as we could.
And, you know, we talked of, obviously, of the immediate problems, and-- and we were in quest of immediate solutions to very real, practical problems, on the one hand.
On the other hand, obviously we talked about the-- the hopes for an early end to the war before we all [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] die right then and there, so to say.
And we talked of resistance, you know.
What could resistance be.

We collected and piled up stones in various areas throughout the ghetto, and then [INAUDIBLE] believe that even when either the opportunity arose or the need arose, we would try to defend ourselves with stones.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We got ourselves, somehow, the so-called police, and not every-- not even-- really I shouldn't even call it that.
We-- we all-- we all got ourselves a big stick.
That sort of was our uniform.
And we were hoping that those sticks are going to come in handy.
We-- some of us had a few axes that we were-- that weren't surrendered or weren't discovered.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a punishment-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] auxiliary, labor auxiliary, in what used to be the hotel, the back yard of which abutted the ghetto.
And we were supposed to [INAUDIBLE].
There was a-- there was a little [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] kind of walkway created by two sets of-- of planks or-- or-- or wood, which we were supposed to-- within which we were supposed to patrol.
And one side was open into that-- the backyard of this [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] former hotel.

And so we communicated-- we had somehow managed to communicate with-- with these more mature people who were in the-- in the camp, and again, attempting to try to devise some fashion or some way of-- of escape and-- and of organizing or resisting.
It was all a very macabre kind of thing because there really was not much reality to it.
There couldn't have been.
We did also prepare a couple of-- a couple of hiding places for people who were willing to-- to hide without-- when-- when we sort of realized and knew that we were going to be deported.

And in fact, just in preparation for this recording, at your request, I was looking at some pictures [INAUDIBLE] against of one of the-- at the pictures of the person who was the [INAUDIBLE] of [INAUDIBLE] in-- in Kisvarda who hid out in one of the places that we prepared and-- and survived and came to-- to Palestine in-- in I think already in '45.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

So [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] yeah, I really think that the way-- the best way for me to describe this-- this nightmare is that it was all of one big macabre dance of death that we were all dancing, some unconsciously and some consciously, or more consciously than others.
But it was really just a-- a dance of death.
Did the average resident in the ghetto have access to outside of the ghetto?
No.
No.
Who at that time was patrolling external-- externally?
It was all the Hungarian gendarme.

As a matter of fact, to the best of my recollection, and although I have learned since then that [INAUDIBLE] apparently had with it at Kisvarda once, there was one young SS man who was guiding, directing, and maybe even commanding the local gendarme.
But they really needed very little guidance, or very little help.
The Hungarian gendarme really was a particularly brutal and-- and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do you call it?
Aggressive and-- and wild institution from its very inception.
And they were in charge, for all intents and purposes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To what extent they were guided by-- bye the Germans is-- is something that I-- I don't know.
But as I say, they needed very little guidance.

Interestingly enough, one of the-- the plans we hatched, the-- the-- the other side of the ghetto or-- or of Star Street, the end of where the Jewish school was-- just behind the Jewish school was, and that was all part of the ghetto.
There were about 12,000 people in the ghetto in Kisvarda, so you can imagine, they were all squeezed into, I would say, within the equivalent of in-- in built up area, of about two blocks.
Maybe three, I don't know.
But, you know, the school and the synagogues and even the slaughter house, everything was filled with people.

But it-- it backed into the officers and the guard room of the gendarmerie, and one of the plans that we were hatching and trying to figure out how we could do it was to break into that guard house and you know, do away with whatever gendarmes you could and-- and get their weapons.
Of course, it never came to it.
We never-- and this is another element and aspect if it.
It holds true in-- in many ways, for other areas, of course, and for the whole question of-- of resistance.
The-- the whole question of time, you know?

Before you could, and perhaps there was something conscience-- conscious about this, and planful on the part of-- of the Germans, there was really never any time to-- to organize or to plan or-- or to carry out any kind of plan.
Before you knew it, the circumstances changed so drastically that, no matter what your plans were, you couldn't-- you couldn't carry them out.
You were already taken away or-- or-- or killed.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is, by the way, one of the very important [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] philosophical, cum practical questions.
The whole concept of time in the Holocaust was something that was beyond the normal human circumstances.
No one really can, who has not been there, can appreciate.
But this is just one really relatively smaller element of it.

But the-- the circumstances were so unpredictable, so unreliable, so swiftly changing that certainly in-- in our ghetto, but-- but every place else in the camps and-- and in the other ghettos, that you could not plan and you could not for sure plan.
We dreamed and-- and attempted, somehow, to make contact with my father and thought maybe he can save us in the last moment.
Of course, we-- we couldn't.
We had, for several years, maintained contact with my father through Switzerland, but you couldn't even post a letter.
And again, if you had posted a letter, by the time it arrived in Switzerland we were burned.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So, you know, discussing is there a way in which we can notify our father and maybe he can do something, some-- invoke some kind of a deus ex machina and save us.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We were, of course, all trying to somehow fortify and-- and save each other, in all sorts of ways that we could.

I had actually left the ghetto once, having being firmly convinced that it was a hopeless situation, and believing that, you know, whoever can should save himself so there will be a [INAUDIBLE] Israel, a remnant.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But, as so many others, I couldn't countenance leaving my family.
So I came back.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I came back voluntarily and have been, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and it's a peculiar word to use for something like that, but I have been happy ever since that I have done it, that I have been able to provide some comfort, some easement, some encouragement, some strength for the rest of my family.
And the same goes for them.
They, I'm sure, felt the same way.
Sorry?
No.
At one time, you know, we bought off the gendarme, who was our guard.
At another time, we snuck one of the boys away, and one time I sneaked away and went into the back door of an apothecary and just broke it open and schlepped out whatever I could.
It was-- it was all very makeshift and very much dependent on the opportunity of the moment.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Everything was like that.

And again, you had-- it's-- I don't know how I can sufficiently impress you with this problem of time, which was a completely different dimension and category than-- than what is normal to-- to people and to human existence.
We were-- we were in the jaws of death every moment.
Sure.
